coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from mangosteen peel for the detection of Cr2O7 2− and vanillin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yaxuan
Guo, Yushi
Xing, Zhiqiang
Bai, Lu
Yang, Wenxiao
Wu, Qiuhua
Zhang, Guolin
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped biomass carbon dots (MP-NCDs) based on mangosteen peel were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. Transmission electron microscopy demonstrated that MP-NCDs were spherical and their particle size ranged from 2.6 to 5.0 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy indicated that they were composed of C, N, and O elements. Fourier transform infrared revealed that their surface was equipped with oxygen-containing and amino-containing groups. Bright blue light emission characteristics were demonstrated. They had high light stability in aqueous solution and salt solution. Furthermore, MP-NCDs could be utilized as a bifunctional fluorescent probe for detecting Cr2O7 2− and vanillin through the fluorescence quenching method, which exhibited high selectivity, sensitivity, and anti-interference performance. The detection limits of vanillin and Cr2O7 2− by MP-NCDs were determined to be 2.33 μM and 4.6 μM, respectively. A detailed analysis was conducted on the fluorescence quenching mechanism. The performance of the sensing system was tested in real samples using river water and infant formula as models.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mangosteen peel
Carbon dots
Fluorescent probe
Vanillin
Cr2O7 2−

Detection of Cr2O7 2− and vanillin in real sample
MP-NCDs for fluorescence detection of Cr2O7 2
MP-NCDs for fluorescent detection of vanillin
Detection of Cr2O7 2− and vanillin in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of synthesis of MP-NCDs.
(a) TEM image of MP-NCDs (Insert: HRTEM image), (b) Histogram of the particle size distribution of MP-NCDs.
(a) XRD pattern of MP-NCDs; (b) FT-IR spectra of MP-NCDs and mangosteen peel.
XPS full survey (a), high-resolution XPS of C 1s (b), N 1s (c) and O 1s (d) spectra of MP-NCDs.
(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of MP-NCDs at various excitation wavelengths; (b) UV and fluorescence spectra of MP-NCDs (illustration: MP-NCDs exhibit blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp).

(a) Fluorescence intensity of MP-NCDs in the presence of different anions, (b) Equilibrium time of MP-NCDs as fluorescence probes for detecting Cr2O7 2−, (c) Fluorescence emission intensity of MP-NCDs at room temperature when Cr2O7 2− and Cr2O7 2− coexist with other ions, (d) Fluorescence spectra of MP-NCDs in the presence of different concentrations of Cr2O7 2−.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectra of MP-NCDs before and after adding vanillin, (b) Equilibrium time of MP-NCDs as fluorescence probes for detecting vanillin, (c) Fluorescence spectra of MP-NCDs in the presence of different concentrations of vanillin, (d) Fluorescence emission intensity of MP-NCDs at room temperature when vanillin and vanillin coexist with other substances.

(a) UV absorption spectra of Cr2O7 2− and vanillin, excitation and emission spectra of MP-NCDs, (b) The fluorescence lifetime decay curves of MP-NCDs before and after the presence of Cr2O7 2− or vanillin.
Detection of Cr2O7 2− in actual samples.
Detection of vanillin in actual samples.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from mangosteen peel for the detection of Cr2O7 2− and vanillin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials (Liaoning University), College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials (Liaoning University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liaoning Provincial Key Laboratory for Green Synthesis and Preparative Chemistry of Advanced Materials (Liaoning University), College of Chemistry, Liaoning University, Shenyang 110036, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped biomass carbon dots (MP-NCDs) were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method using mangosteen peel. MP-NCDs can be used as a bifunctional fluorescent probe to detect Cr2O7 2− and vanillin by fluorescence quenching method, with high selectivity, high sensitivity and anti-interference performance.

Nitrogen-doped biomass carbon dots (MP-NCDs) based on mangosteen peel were prepared by one-step hydrothermal method. Transmission electron microscopy demonstrated that MP-NCDs were spherical and their particle size ranged from 2.6 to 5.0 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy indicated that they were composed of C, N, and O elements. Fourier transform infrared revealed that their surface was equipped with oxygen-containing and amino-containing groups. Bright blue light emission characteristics were demonstrated. They had high light stability in aqueous solution and salt solution. Furthermore, MP-NCDs could be utilized as a bifunctional fluorescent probe for detecting Cr2O7 2− and vanillin through the fluorescence quenching method, which exhibited high selectivity, sensitivity, and anti-interference performance. The detection limits of vanillin and Cr2O7 2− by MP-NCDs were determined to be 2.33 μM and 4.6 μM, respectively. A detailed analysis was conducted on the fluorescence quenching mechanism. The performance of the sensing system was tested in real samples using river water and infant formula as models.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution by heavy metals has become increasingly serious in recent years. Many industrial wastewaters contain excessive metal ions, such as Cr(VI) [1,2]. In contaminated water, Cr(VI) is typically present in the form of Cr2O7 2−. It exhibits extreme toxicity and carcinogenic effects on plants and humans, posing a significant threat to life in the environment [3–5]. Therefore, the simple and inexpensive sensing technology for high-sensitivity detection of Cr(VI) levels in water has received great attention. Vanillin is a naturally occurring plant spice with unique aroma and often used to enhance flavor of milk products including infant formulas [6,7]. Chronic consumption of vanillin might result in nausea, vomiting, and even liver and kidney dysfunction [8–10]. GB2760-2014 has stated that no food flavors should be added to infant formula food for children aged 0 to 6 months [11]. Consequently, the rapid and effective analysis and detection of vanillin in infant formula has aroused the interest of scientists.

Currently, a variety of detection methods have emerged to identify Cr(VI) and vanillin, such as high-performance liquid chromatography [12,13] and inductively coupled plasma emission spectroscopy [14], capillary electrophoresis [15,16], electrochemical [17] and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectroscopy [18], etc. Although these methods usually give satisfactory results, their applications are limited to a certain extent due to shortcomings such as lengthy procedures, complex preprocessing, costly instrumentation, and unstable electrode signals. On the other hand, fluorescence detection has emerged as the most promising substitute for these technologies because of its affordability, high sensitivity, simplicity, outstanding stability, and quick reaction time [19–21].

As a new nanomaterial in the nanocarbon family, carbon dots have attracted great attention in recent years [22–24]. As an emerging fluorescent material, they are considered an ideal material for fluorescent probe detection due to their excellent fluorescence stability, photobleaching resistance and long fluorescence lifetime [25,26]. Nevertheless, there are still several challenges to overcome, including complex preparation processes, strict synthesis conditions, and potential environmental pollution. Utilizing low-cost or renewable biomass waste as a carbon source for preparation environmentally friendly carbon dots has become an inevitable trend of environmentally sustainable development [27,28]. Furthermore, to reduce resource consumption and energy waste, the simultaneous detection of two or more targets that do not exist in the same system has become a research focus for investigators. Mangosteen, a fruit extensively cultivated in Southeast Asia, as well as in Yunnan and Hainan in China, presents an interesting prospect. The peel of mangosteen accounts for approximately two-thirds of its total weight, and is often discarded as waste, leading to resource wastage and environmental pollution [29]. Therefore, employing mangosteen peel for the synthesis of carbon dots offers a viable solution to the problem of food waste utilization.

In this work, biomass mangosteen peel was used as the carbon source, and ethylenediamine was employed as the nitrogen source, to synthesize nitrogen-doped blue luminescent carbon dots (MP-NCDs) derived from mangosteen peel. This synthesis, conducted under hydrothermal conditions, yielded a high percentage of 21 %. MP-NCDs had excellent fluorescence properties and were used as a fluorescent probe to detect Cr(VI) and vanillin. The fluorescence quenching mechanism was leveraging Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) and dynamic quenching. Furthermore, the spiked recovery experiments verified the potential of MP-NCDs in detecting Cr(VI) and vanillin in practical applications.

Ethylenediamine (EDA), ethanol, vanillin, potassium bromide (KBr), potassium chloride (KCl), potassium carbonate (K2CO3), potassium bicarbonate (KHCO3), potassium hydrogen phosphate (KHPO4), potassium nitrate (KNO3), potassium hydroxide (KOH), potassium persulfate (K2S2O8), potassium sulfate (K2SO4), potassium dichromate (KCr2O7), potassium iodide (KI), manganese chloride (MnCl2), calcium chloride (CaCl2), copper chloride (CuCl2), manganese chloride (MgCl2), Zinc chloride (ZnCl2), nickel chloride (NiCl2), barium chloride (BaCl2), cadmium chloride (CdCl2), lead chloride (PbCl₂), mercuric chloride (HgCl₂), taurine, inositol, citric acid, niacin, ascorbic acid, benzoic acid, picric acid, urea, vitamins A, B, and E. The raw materials are all analytically pure from commercial sources. Unless otherwise stated, all chemicals and reagents do not need to be further purified and used directly. The dialysis bag was purchased from Shanghai Bowman Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Samples of mangosteen and milk powder were purchased from supermarkets (Shenyang, China). In the conduct of experiments, ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 megohms per centimeter (MΩ·cm−1) was utilized.

The morphology of MP-NCDs was observed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100 UHR) at an accelerated voltage of 200 kV. The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the samples were recorded using a Nicolet 5700 infrared spectrometer (Thermo, USA). The UV–vis spectrum was recorded using a TU-1900 ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy experiments were performed using Thermo ESCALAB 250. The crystal phase composition was determined on an X-ray diffractometer (D8-Advance X) with a test range of 5–50°. The Zeta potential was tested on the Nano-ZS nanoparticle size and Zeta potential analyzer (Kruss, UK). Fluorescence tests were performed on a Cary Eclipse 300 fluorescence spectrophotometer. The fluorescence lifetime of MP-NCDs was recorded by a FLS1000 steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer.

In this experiment, MP-NCDs were prepared by traditional hydrothermal synthesis. Firstly, the mangosteen peel was washed twice with ethanol and ultrapure water, respectively. After drying under 60 °C, it was ground into powder. In the preparation of MP-NCDs, 5.0 g mangosteen peel powder was mixed with 30 mL ultrapure water and 0.5 mL ethylenediamine. The mixture was stirred evenly under a magnetic stirrer and further sonicated for 10 min. Subsequently, the mixed suspension was moved a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave lined with polytetrafluoroethylene and maintained at 200 °C for 12 h. When the temperature dropped to the environment, the unreacted components and small molecule impurities was removed by centrifugation (12000 rpm, 10 min), filtration (polyethersulfone filter membrane), and dialysis (MWCO=100, 48 h and change water every 6 h). The collected carbon dots solution was freeze-dried at −50 °C for 48 h to obtain MP-NCDs powder, which was subsequently stored in a refrigerator at 4 °C for further analysis and potential applications. For comparison, undoped MP-CDs were prepared following the same experimental procedure without adding ethylenediamine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of Cr2O7 2− and vanillin
ce:section.ce:para: The parameters of the fluorescence spectrophotometer were set as follows: excitation voltage was set to 600 V, with both the excitation and emission slits calibrated to 10 nm each. All reactions and measurements were conducted at ambient temperature. A fluorescence assay for Cr2O7 2− and vanillin under the following procedures. In short, 1 mL of solution (varying in concentration) was introduced into 2 mL of MP-NCDs (0.05 mg/mL) solution, and the fluorescence spectrum was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 446 nm. In the selectivity test, other ions and substances were used instead of Cr2O7 2− or vanillin, and the fluorescence spectrum was measured under the same conditions. The calculation method for the limit of detection (LOD) was detailed in the Supplementary Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr2O7 2− and vanillin in real sample
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the capability of MP-NCDs in detecting of Cr2O7 2− and vanillin in actual samples, the MP-NCDs sensor was used to quantitatively analyze Cr2O7 2− in river water and vanillin in milk powder. The water from the Hunhe River in Shenyang City was filtered by the 0.22 μm microporous membrane to eliminate insoluble substances and impurities. The infant milk powder sample was procured from a nearby supermarket in Shenyang City, Liaoning Province. 5.0 g milk powder sample was dissolved in ultrapure water at 50-60 °C. After cooling, the sample was moved to a 50 mL volumetric flask and ultrapure water was added to scale line. 1 mL of river water or milk powder samples containing varying concentrations of analytes were introduced into 2 mL of MP-NCDs (0.05 mg/mL) solution, and the fluorescence spectrum was recorded at an excitation wavelength of 446 nm after thorough mixing. Each sample was analyzed in triplicate, and the sensitivity of MP-NCDs fluorescent probe to detect Cr2O7 2− and vanillin in real samples was verified by standard addition recovery method.

Using mangosteen peel, which is rich in tannic acid, dietary fiber and flavonoids as a precursor for carbon dots, MP-NCDs were synthesized by a green and inexpensive hydrothermal method (Fig. 1 ). These components are rich in C, H, and O elements, which can provide abundant functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups during the formation of carbon dots.

The undoped carbon dots MP-CDs were prepared following the same procedure. The fluorescence intensity of MP-CDs at 446 nm was much lower than that of MP-NCDs (Fig. S1), implying that the doping of N element significantly improved the fluorescence intensity of MP-NCDs.

The carbonization degree of mangosteen depended on temperature and affected the properties of MP-NCDs. Therefore, the effect of reaction temperature (140 °C, 160 °C, 180 °C, 200 °C, 220 °C) on the fluorescence properties of MP-NCDs was studied. As the reaction temperature increased (140 °C-220 °C), the fluorescence intensity of MP-NCDs first raised, and then lessened (Fig. S2). Its maximum value was at a reaction temperature of 200 °C. Lower temperature resulted in incomplete functionalization and carbonization of MP-NCDs surface [30]. Higher temperatures led to the overly high degree of carbonization and the formation of MP-NCDs with an increased number of carbon nuclei. Then, 200 °C was selected as the optimal reaction temperature. The reactions with various times (6, 8, 10, 12, 14 h) were carried out and MP-NCDs were obtained. The fluorescence intensity of MP-NCDs (Fig. S2) increased with the reaction time progressed between 6 and 12 h, peaking at 12 h, and gradually decreasing after that. Consequently, the preparation time for MP-NCDs was set to 12 h.

The microstructure can be directly observed by TEM. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the MP-NCDs were approximately spherical and well dispersed. HRTEM (Insert of Fig. 2a) demonstrated that the lattice spacing was 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) crystal plane of the graphitized carbon structure [31]. The particle size histogram (Fig. 2b) manifested that the size of MP-NCDs were in the range of 2.6–5.0 nm and its average was 3.6 nm.

The structure of MP-NCDs was explored by XRD characterization (Fig. 3 a). The broad peak at 2θ = 20.8° corresponded to the (100) crystal plane of carbon [32], further confirming that the prepared carbon dots have a graphene structure, which is consistent with the aforementioned HRTEM result.

The surface functional groups of MP-NCDs were characterized by FT-IR (Fig. 3b). The characteristic peak at 3430 cm−1 was attributed to the stretching vibration of O-H/N-H. The characteristic peaks of C-H stretching vibration and C-H out-of-plane bending vibration appeared at 2814 cm−1 and 766 cm−1. The strong absorption peak near 1630 cm−1 came from the stretching vibration of C=C [33]. The peak at 1356 cm−1 derived from C-N stretching vibration was stronger than that of mangosteen peel, indicating that the N in ethylenediamine was successfully doped into MP-NCDs.

The XPS spectra of MP-NCDs provided information on their elemental composition and chemical composition. The XPS full-spectrum scan (Fig. 4 a) revealed that there were three main signal peaks at 286 eV, 407 eV and 533 eV, corresponding to C 1s, N 1s and O 1s peaks, respectively. Their contents were 66.32 %, 5.84 % and 27.84 %, respectively. The characteristic peaks observed at 284.0 eV, 285.4 eV and 287.5 eV in the C 1s spectrum (Fig. 4b) belonged to C-C, C-O and C=O groups, respectively. The N 1s fitting results showed that the two characteristic peaks at 399.9 eV and 401.7 eV corresponded to the C-N-C and N-H groups (Fig. 4c), respectively. The characteristic peaks of the high-resolution energy spectrum of O 1s (Fig. 4d) indicated that C=O (531.4 eV) and C-O-C/C-OH (532.7 eV) were the main forms of O in MP-NCDs [34].

The fluorescence emission spectra under various excitation wavelengths were collected. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the emission peak of MP-NCDs gradually red-shifts tendency as the excitation wavelength increases. The optimal excitation wavelength was 356 nm and the optimal emission wavelength was 446 nm (Fig. 5b). MP-NCDs emitted bright blue fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp (Insert of Fig. 5b). This fluorescence excitation-dependent property might be due to the influence of surface states on the band structure of MP-NCDs caused by the particle size and complex chemical groups of MP-NCDs [35]. In addition, the Φ S of MP-NCDs was calculated to be 12 % by using 0.1 M quinine sulfate solution (quantum yield 54 %) as a reference, and the specific calculation process was shown in the Supplementary Information. The absorption peak at 280 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 5b blue line) was related to the π-π* transition of aromatic carbon nuclei. The strong absorption peak at 321 nm was attributed to the n-π* transition of the N-containing functional group [36].

The effects of pH, UV irradiation time, and salt concentration on the fluorescence intensity were studied. The fluorescence intensity (Fig. S3a) of MP-NCDs scaled up with pH range of 2–6, and remained stable under neutral conditions (pH 7–10). Only under strong alkali (pH=12) conditions, the fluorescence intensity decreased slightly with pH, showing excellent pH stability. The surface of MP-NCDs was rich in –COOH, which formed hydrogen bonds in a strong acid environment, and the hydrogen bonds were destroyed under the alkali environment, resulting in the change of surface state and fluorescence intensity. As shown in Fig. S3b, after continuous irradiation under ultraviolet light for 120 min, the fluorescence intensity of MP-NCDs was only 5 % lower than the original fluorescence intensity, indicating that MP-NCDs had excellent anti-photobleaching ability. The aqueous solution of MP-NCDs containing salt (the concentrations of salt were in the range of 0.2–1.0 M) displayed similar fluorescence intensity as the aqueous solution without salt, showing fine stability in salt solution. The excellent stability under various pH, UV irradiation, and salt solutions provided unlimited possibilities for their application as sensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MP-NCDs for fluorescence detection of Cr2O7 2
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence emission spectra of MP-NCDs were recorded after the addition of ions (Br−, Cl−, CO3 2–, HCO3 –, HPO4 −, NO3 –, OH–, S2O8 2−, SO4 2− or Cr2O7 2−). Fig. 6 a showed that the fluorescence intensity significantly decreases only after adding Cr2O7 2−. Fig. 6b displayed that the fluorescence intensity of MP-NCDs solution decreased rapidly within 17 s and remained stable within 300 s after the addition of Cr2O7 2− ions. Thus, MP-NCDs could be used as a luminescent probe with rapid responsiveness for the detection of Cr2O7 2−. Fig. 6c demonstrated that the existence of interfering substances barely influenced the detection of Cr2O7 2−, indicating the good anti-interference ability of the luminescent probe.

A concentration gradient titration experiment was carried out (Fig. 6d). The observed data indicated a progressive diminution in fluorescence intensity as the concentration of Cr2O7 2− increased. Specifically, at a concentration of 0.3 mM, the fluorescence quenching efficiency was 97 %. By the Stern-Volmer equation, the KSV constant was 1.238 × 104 M (Insert of Fig. 6d), and the LOD was 4.6 × 10−6 M.

ce:section.ce:section-title: MP-NCDs for fluorescent detection of vanillin
ce:section.ce:para: The detection of vanillin using MP-NCDs was also explored. Fig. 7 a showed that the addition of vanillin resulted in fluorescence quenching. Fig. 7b demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity of MP-NCDs decreased sharply within 13 s and remained stable within 300 s, indicating that the fluorescent probe responded quickly. As the concentration of vanillin increased, the fluorescence intensity of MP-NCDs gradually decreased (Fig. 7c). When the addition amount of vanillin reached 175 μM, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of MP-NCDs was about 90 %. The quenching constant KSV calculated by the Stern-Volmer equation was 2.352 × 104 M. The quenching curve showed a good linear correlation in the range of low concentration of vanillin (Insert of Fig. 7c). The LOD of MP-NCDs for detecting vanillin was 2.33 × 10−6 M.

The addition of interference substances (K+, Na+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, OH–, I−, taurine, inositol, citric acid, Cl−, nicotinic acid, ascorbic acid, vitamin A, vitamin B, vitamin E, benzoic acid, picric acid and urea) did not result in changes in fluorescence intensity (Fig. 7d), indicating that MP-NCDs had excellent anti-interference ability and could be used for the determination of vanillin in simulated milk powder.

Common fluorescence quenching mechanisms were inner filter effect (IFE), static quenching, dynamic quenching, FRET and photoinduced electron transfer (PET), etc. As shown in Fig. 8 a, the UV–vis spectra of the two analytes overlapped with the excitation spectrum of MP-NCDs, which proved that FRET or IFE might exist in the process of fluorescence quenching.

The fluorescence lifetime was investigated (Fig. 8). The fluorescence lifetime of MP-NCDs without vanillin or Cr2O7 2− (τ1) was 9.751 ns and those with vanillin (τ2) or Cr2O7 2− (τ3) were 8.196 ns, and 5.469 ns, respectively. The decrease in fluorescence lifetime after addition of analytes suggested that the fluorescence quenching mechanism was not attributed to static quenching or IFE, but dynamic quenching. No new absorption peaks emerged in the UV–Vis spectra (Fig. S4) of MP-NCDs after addition of analytes suggested that no new substances were formed, corroborating the occurrence of a dynamic quenching process.

The sufficient proximity between fluorescent groups and quenchers is a necessary condition for FRET to occur [37]. The Zeta potential of MP-NCDs was −11.4 mV at pH 7.0 (Fig. S5), and it increased to 0.30 mV and 1.05 mV after the addition of Cr2O7 2− or vanillin, respectively. The results suggested that MP-NCDs could approach the analytes (Cr2O7 2− or vanillin) through electrostatic interactions, providing the possibility for FRET. Upon absorbing photons of a particular frequency, the donor molecules (MP-NCDs) were excited to a higher electronic energy state. Before the electron returned to its ground state, the energy from the donor's excited state was transferred to the excited state of the acceptor molecules (Cr2O7 2− or vanillin) through intermolecular electric dipole interactions, resulting in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of the donor (Fig. S6). Notably, despite receiving the energy and attaining its excited state, the acceptor molecules failed to directly emit this energy as fluorescence. As a result, a phenomenon of fluorescence quenching was observed. Thus, the fluorescence quenching of MP-NCDs by Cr2O7 2− and vanillin was facilitated by the synergistic effect of FRET and dynamic quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cr2O7 2− and vanillin in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Additionally, a comparison between the fluorescent sensor developed in this study and other analytical methods for Cr2O7 2− and vanillin detection was conducted. As shown in Table S1, the LOD of MP-NCDs was comparable to those reported in the literature. Different methods have their respective advantages and disadvantages in terms of response time, preprocessing steps, and identification accuracy. Notably, the detection method employed in this study was user-friendly and significantly reduced the detection time, demonstrating promising prospects for rapid detection of Cr2O7 2− and vanillin in complex samples.

In summary, the blue fluorescent MP-NCDs with excellent optical properties were prepared by hydrothermal synthesis using biomass waste mangosteen peel as a precursor. The carbon dots exhibited uniform size, remarkable stability and excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior in aqueous solution. Due to the synergistic effect of FRET and dynamic quenching, MP-NCDs served as a specific recognition fluorescent probe for Cr2O7 2− and vanillin. The probe had high selectivity and sensitivity, enabling the detection of Cr2O7 2− and vanillin in actual samples. The spiked recovery and standard deviation were satisfactory. This work provided significant application value for the rapid analysis of complex samples.

Yaxuan Chen: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. Yushi Guo: Conceptualization. Zhiqiang Xing: Conceptualization. Lu Bai: Formal analysis. Wenxiao Yang: Formal analysis. Qiuhua Wu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Guolin Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Natural Science Foundation for Education Department of Liaoning Province of China (No. LJC201906) and Start-up funds for doctoral research of Liaoning University (a280008030).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2024.115945.
